chunk_summary: Guidelines for interpreting ferritin levels in children, considering inflammation markers like CRP/AGP, to avoid misdiagnosing iron deficiency in clinical settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ferritin as an iron store marker and its limitations during inflammation.
Learn the ferritin cutoff values for diagnosing iron deficiency in children with and without inflammation.
Identify appropriate clinical actions based on ferritin levels and inflammation status.
Recognize when to prioritize hemoglobinopathy evaluation over iron supplementation.
key_facts: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and must be interpreted alongside inflammation markers (CRP/AGP).
Ferritin <12 micrograms/L (children <5 years) or <15 micrograms/L (children ≥5 years) indicates iron deficiency without inflammation.
Ferritin <30 micrograms/L is a pragmatic cutoff for iron deficiency in the presence of inflammation.
Do not order ferritin alone during acute febrile illness; elevated ferritin may falsely suggest iron sufficiency.
In microcytosis with low ferritin, treat iron deficiency and address underlying causes like dietary issues or deworming.
For microcytosis with non-low ferritin and active inflammation, recheck after recovery rather than escalating to parenteral iron.
reference_quote: Ferritin concentrations for the assessment of iron status and iron deficiency — When ferritin is intermediate and inflammation is present, phenotype and response planning become more valuable than chasing mul.
document_key_topics: Ferritin as a marker of iron stores
Impact of inflammation on ferritin interpretation
Clinical guidelines for diagnosing iron deficiency in children
Management strategies for microcytosis and anemia
